elements.name: Title
texts: "They would make a Jonas of me against my will"

elements.name: Description
texts: Knox reasons that the boatmen would prefer that the monster eat the heretic reverend rather than them

elements.name: Source
texts: Pastoral Annals. By an Irish Clergyman [i.e. James S. Knox], p. 391=2

elements.name: Publisher
texts: R. B. Seeley and W. Burnside, London

elements.name: Contributor
texts: Digitised by Google, sponsored by Princeton Library

elements.name: Text
texts: "Two things were now clear to me - First, that the panic of these fellows [about the lake serpent] might be sincere; and secondly, that if it were, they had comforted themselves with the conclusion, that if the offended honour of the monster required a sacrifice, the heretic parson would undoubtedly be selected. I felt quite sure that such had been the process of induction adopted by my fellow reasoners; so, entertaining no fears, either that they would make a Jonas of me against my will, or that the Lake King desired my for an 'entremet,' I plied my art with so much diligence, that in a few minutes we were alongside the object of my companions' fear."
